briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for May 22, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Urgent Warning: Intensifying Climate Change Impacts and Vulnerabilities
sections.content: Domestic and international news released today once again reminds us of the severe impacts of climate change. Domestically, plants at the peak of Seoraksan Mountain are reacting most rapidly to climate change, serving as a significant indicator of ongoing changes [1]. Furthermore, over the past 60 years, greenhouse gas emissions, particularly methane, from rice paddies have doubled, revealing an increasing contribution from the agricultural sector [2, 54]. Internationally, widespread environmental changes are being detected, including salinity intrusion issues in agriculture in the Mekong Delta region [4], decreasing oxygen levels in rivers worldwide [56], localized temperature increases due to data centers [55], and rapid drought-flood weather fluctuations [49]. The UK Climate Change Committee warned that the country was 'built for a climate that no longer exists,' emphasizing the urgency of adaptation policies [43, 58]. Retreats in climate action, such as the Trump administration's rollback of refrigerant regulations for food [42], continue to exacerbate the risks of climate change [38]. These warnings clearly demonstrate that the climate crisis is not merely an environmental issue but a complex threat encompassing all aspects of our lives [52].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Efforts and the Challenges of Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to achieve carbon neutrality goals. The Korea Chamber of Commerce and Industry and Korea Southern Power have expanded their carbon neutrality cooperation to jointly address the energy transition in the industrial sector [9, 11, 24, 27]. Geoje City, in collaboration with Samsung Heavy Industries and Geoje University, is actively working to spread a culture of carbon neutrality practices [7, 13, 15, 20, 21, 25, 26]. Furthermore, Gurye County is preparing for the 'Carbon Neutrality Soil Revitalization Expo' [14], and Busan Nam-gu is operating the 'UN Nam-gu Recycling Experience Center' to encourage carbon neutrality practices in daily life [16], highlighting concrete attempts at the local government level. A research team at Sungkyunkwan University has developed a next-generation catalyst that produces high-efficiency, eco-friendly ethanol from greenhouse gas carbon dioxide, presenting the possibility of technological solutions [23]. However, despite Gwangju City showing a clear achievement of 94% in its carbon neutrality goal [18], the persistent issue of severe traffic congestion [17] indicates that additional efforts are needed for substantial change. Meanwhile, the abolition of the RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standard) in the renewable energy market and its complete overhaul to a 'long-term auction system' starting next year [31] signals significant changes in energy transition policies. Issues such as Busan's low renewable energy self-sufficiency rate [33] and environmental groups' opposition to the Saemangeum nuclear power plant proposal [30] reveal the complex challenges of domestic energy policy.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community: Climate Change Response Obligations and Policy Landscape
sections.content: The international community is reaffirming the importance of responding to climate change and continuing its policy efforts. The UN General Assembly adopted a resolution confirming states' obligations regarding climate change response, emphasizing the need for international cooperation [40, 65]. The International Court of Justice (ICJ) also issued an advisory opinion on climate change [3, 44], which is expected to significantly influence the ecological and industrial policy transitions of various nations. Concurrently, the importance of climate risk assessment within economic systems is being highlighted, with Federal Reserve investigators macroscopically studying climate change and analyzing its unequal impacts [47]. Conversely, analyses suggesting that the Italian Meloni government is paying the price for an energy crisis as its green transition policies halt [36], and conservative movements urging the Supreme Court to dismiss climate change damage lawsuits [48], illustrate the uncertain landscape of international climate action. Amidst this complex situation, new technological approaches are gaining attention as policy alternatives, such as AI emerging as a key tool for managing the UK's clean energy transition [62].

sections.title: 🌱 Innovation and Adaptation: New Approaches in the Era of Climate Crisis
sections.content: Innovative attempts and adaptation strategies are being explored from various angles to respond to the era of climate crisis. The case of discarded school meals meeting artificial intelligence (AI) technology, leading to sharing and carbon neutrality practices [12], demonstrates the potential for creating social and environmental value through technology. Furthermore, applications for consulting support to reduce heating and cooling costs and achieve carbon neutrality for buildings over 10 years old have begun [19], indicating active efforts to reduce carbon through improving the efficiency of existing infrastructure. In academia, a research team at Sungkyunkwan University has developed a next-generation single-atom catalyst that produces high-efficiency, eco-friendly ethanol from the greenhouse gas carbon dioxide (CO2) [23], leading advancements in carbon capture and utilization technologies. Experts are discussing migration in the era of warming not as a 'failure of adaptation' but as 'a form of adaptation' [66], presenting a new perspective on climate migration. These diverse innovation and adaptation efforts strengthen our capacity to respond to climate change and open a hopeful path towards a sustainable future.

briefing.closing: Through today's briefing, we have been able to confirm the severity of climate change, along with various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. Continued attention and action will create a better future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
